title: Finite-size occupancy scaling of apparent fractal dimensions in stochastic trajectories
abstract: Estimating a fractal dimension from a finite stochastic trajectory is a finite-size scaling problem: the apparent box-counting exponent is shaped by an occupancy crossover between the resolved range of scales and the finite number of sampled points, and need not equal the dimension of the limiting process. We model this crossover with a balls-in-boxes occupancy law, which predicts the box-count curve, the finite-size saturation scale, and a scaling function for the normalized local slope. Across random-walk traces, fractional Brownian graphs, and Levy flights, the normalized local slope collapses onto a single crossover curve, while the windowed box-counting bias collapses when the regression window is positioned relative to the saturation scale. Inverting the occupancy model gives a finite-size bias correction that reduces error on controlled stochastic trajectories and transfers across held-out model classes. Comparisons with correlation dimension, detrended fluctuation analysis, the variogram, and Higuchi's method show that the dominant bias is specific to point-sampled box-counting over finite scale windows, and that local-slope stability alone is not a reliable diagnostic. A DNA-walk example illustrates the workflow on measured data, and all figures, tables, and in-text numbers are regenerated from released single-seed code.
summary: This paper addresses the systematic error in calculating fractal dimensions from finite datasets. By modeling the error as a finite-size scaling problem using a balls-in-boxes approach, the authors achieve a universal correction that works across different types of random processes.
main_problem: Estimating the fractal dimension from finite stochastic trajectories leads to a systematic bias in the box-counting exponent due to an occupancy crossover between resolved scales and finite sample points.
main_result: The authors demonstrate a universal scaling collapse for the box-counting bias and local slope across diverse processes (random walks, fBm, Levy flights) and provide an invertible occupancy model that corrects dimension estimates.
method: A 'balls-in-boxes' occupancy model is used to treat the crossover as a finite-size scaling problem, validated through large-scale simulations and application to DNA walk data.
model_or_system: The study analyzes various stochastic trajectories, including Simple Random Walks, fractional Brownian motion graphs, and Levy flights, using a mean-field balls-in-boxes occupancy framework.
key_observables: Fractal dimension (Df), box-count curve, normalized local slope, and windowed box-counting bias.
important_parameters: Saturation scale (k*), number of effective sampled sites (Neff), Hurst exponent (H), and the scaling variables (xi and x).
main_assumptions: The model uses a mean-field approximation treating trajectory points as independently distributed into cells, which may fail for multifractal or strongly anisotropic systems.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the predicted box-count curve; Figure 2 shows the collapse of windowed bias against a scaling variable; Figure 3 shows the collapse of normalized local slopes; Figure 4 demonstrates the effectiveness of the bias correction on held-out models.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the finite-size bias problem, presents the balls-in-boxes occupancy model, demonstrates universal scaling collapse across different trajectory classes, validates the model with a bias correction, and applies the workflow to E. coli DNA walks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Measured box-count scaling (markers) and the occupancy model (2) (solid, Df = 2, one fitted prefactor) for SRW at two d (N = 220). Dotted lines mark the fitted k⋆; the shaded band is the default window.
Figure 2: Scaling collapse of the box-counting bias against x = kmid −1

Figure 3: Cross-family data collapse. Normalized local slope s(k)/Df versus the crossover variable ξ = Df(k −k⋆) = log2(b/Neff) for walk traces, fBm graphs, and Lévy flights, with the scaling function h(ξ) of Eq. (4) (black). Points are the dyadic local slope of the data; the quantitative agreement is measured against the model’s matched finite-difference slope.

Figure 4: Bias correction. (a) RMSE of the windowed box-counting estimate versus the occupancy- fit dimension, by family. (b) Fit-loss surface (log10 mean-squared error) in the occupancy param- eters (Df, A) for a single d = 2 walk trace; the single well-defined minimum (red star; dotted line at the true Df = 2) shows the two-parameter fit is identifiable from one trajectory. (c) Held-out master-curve correction ( bF(x) trained on three walk models, applied to two others).

Figure 5: Estimator comparison. Left: walk traces (true dimension 2), box-counting versus cor- relation dimension. Right: fBm graphs (true dimension 2 −H), all five estimators; DFA and the variogram estimate the exponent H and are plotted as the implied graph dimension 2 −bH.
